skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate AI videos with Google Veo, Seedance 2.0, HappyHorse, Wan, Grok and 40+ models via inference.sh CLI. Models: Veo 3.1, Veo 3, Seedance 2.0, HappyHorse 1.0, Wan 2.5, Grok Imagine Video, OmniHuman, Fabric, HunyuanVideo. Capabilities: text-to-video, image-to-video, reference-to-video, video editing, lipsync, avatar animation, video upscaling, foley sound. Use for: social media videos, marketing content, explainer videos, product demos, AI avatars. Triggers: video generation, ai video, text to video, image to video, veo, animate image, video from image, ai animation, video generator, generate video, t2v, i2v, ai video maker, create video with ai, runway alternative, pika alternative, sora alternative, kling alternative, seedance, happyhorse
skill.summary: Generate AI videos with 40+ models (Veo, Seedance, HappyHorse, Wan, Grok) using the inference.sh belt CLI for text-to-video, image-to-video, lipsync, and upscaling.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill is a markdown documentation file (SKILL.md) that describes how to use the inference.sh belt CLI for AI video generation. All 63 'shell backtick execution' detections are false positives — they are markdown fenced code blocks (```bash) containing example CLI commands, not actual shell execution. The 20 'hardcoded URL' findings are documentation links (e.g., inference.sh docs, image CDN URLs) rather than exfiltration targets. The 2 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings at lines 3 and 75 are false positives — line 3 is the description metadata and line 75 is a table header; neither contains cryptographic code. The skill is a documentation wrapper for a legitimate video generation platform with no evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External CLI tool invocation
low_findings.description: The skill invokes the `belt` CLI (an external command from inference.sh) via Bash. While this is the intended functionality of the skill and uses a scoped `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)` permission, users should be aware it executes external commands that make network requests to the inference.sh platform.

low_findings.title: Network calls to external service
low_findings.description: The skill requires connecting to the inference.sh API to generate videos. This is the core functionality but means user prompts and uploaded media (images, audio) are sent to a third-party service. Users should review inference.sh's privacy policy.

content.value_statement: Creating professional AI videos normally requires juggling multiple platforms and APIs. This skill provides a unified interface to generate videos using Veo, Seedance, HappyHorse, Wan, Grok, and 40+ other models through a single CLI.
actual_capabilities: Generate text-to-video using models like Google Veo 3.1, Seedance 2.0, and HappyHorse
Animate still images with image-to-video models including Wan 2.5 and OmniHuman
Create AI talking-head avatars with lipsync using Fabric, OmniHuman, or PixVerse
Edit existing videos with natural language prompts using HappyHorse Video Edit
Upscale video resolution using Topaz Video Upscaler
Add AI-generated foley sound effects to silent videos using HunyuanVideo Foley
limitations: Requires a separate inference.sh account and CLI installation
Video generation quality and duration vary by model and pricing tier
Some models have resolution caps (480p/720p/1080p) depending on the provider
Cannot generate videos offline — requires active network connection to inference.sh API

use_cases.title: Social media content creator
use_cases.description: Quickly produce short-form AI videos for TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts using text prompts or image inputs.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

use_cases.title: Marketing team video production
use_cases.description: Generate product demos, explainer videos, and promotional clips without hiring a video production team.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing teams and small business owners

use_cases.title: Developer building video pipelines
use_cases.description: Integrate AI video generation into automated content workflows and applications via the belt CLI.

prompt_templates.title: Basic text-to-video
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a short video of a drone flying over a misty forest at sunrise using Veo 3.1 Fast.

prompt_templates.title: Image-to-video animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Take this landscape photo (https://example.com/photo.jpg) and animate gentle wind movement through the trees using Wan 2.5.

prompt_templates.title: AI avatar with lipsync
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking head video using this portrait (https://example.com/face.jpg) and this audio narration (https://example.com/speech.mp3) with OmniHuman 1.5.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-model video pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a 10-second Seedance 2.0 video of a jazz band with audio, then upscale to 1080p with Topaz, and add foley sound effects using HunyuanVideo Foley.

output_examples.input: User prompt: 'Generate a timelapse of a flower blooming in a garden'
output: Command: belt app run google/veo-3-1-fast --input '{"prompt": "A timelapse of a flower blooming in a garden"}'
Output: Video file saved to disk with progress streaming
Duration: ~30 seconds generation time for 5-second clip

output_examples.input: User prompt: 'Animate my product photo with a rotating camera effect'
output: Output: Animated MP4 with smooth camera motion

best_practices: Run `belt login` once before first use to authenticate with inference.sh
Use the Fast model variants (e.g., veo-3-1-fast) for quick iteration, then switch to quality models for final renders
Always specify aspect ratio and resolution explicitly to avoid unexpected defaults
anti_patterns: Do not pass large video files as direct base64 — use public URLs or file paths for image/video inputs
Do not chain more than 3 model calls in a single pipeline without testing each step independently first
Do not use the most expensive model for initial tests — start with fast/economical variants

faq.question: Do I need to pay for inference.sh to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You need an inference.sh account with credits. Pricing varies by model — fast models are cheaper, high-quality models cost more per generation.

faq.question: Which model should I use for the best quality?
faq.answer: Google Veo 3.1 and Seedance 2.0 are the top choices for quality. Veo 3.1 offers frame interpolation; Seedance 2.0 supports synchronized audio.

faq.question: Can I generate videos from my own images?
faq.answer: Yes. Use any image-to-video model like Wan 2.5 or HappyHorse I2V. Pass a public image URL in the image_url or image parameter.

faq.question: Is the belt CLI safe to install?
faq.answer: The belt CLI is an official tool from inference.sh. Install it via the documented method in the skill (npx skills add belt-sh/cli) and authenticate with belt login.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as listed in the supported tools.

faq.question: What video formats are supported?
faq.answer: Output is typically MP4. Use the Media Merger tool to combine multiple clips with transitions like fade or cut.
